title: Quiz: Data Encoding Strategies
description: Test your understanding of quantum data encoding methods

questions.question: How many qubits does amplitude encoding require for a vector of N features?
questions.explanation: Amplitude encoding stores N values in the amplitudes of log₂(N) qubits, achieving exponential compression of the data.

questions.question: What is the main advantage of angle encoding over amplitude encoding?
questions.explanation: Angle encoding requires only single-qubit rotations, making state preparation simple and shallow, unlike amplitude encoding which needs complex multi-qubit operations.

questions.question: In data re-uploading, what is the key idea?

options.text: Use more qubits for better accuracy
Encode data once at the beginning
Encode data multiple times throughout the circuit

questions.explanation: Data re-uploading repeatedly encodes the input data at multiple layers, alternating with trainable quantum layers, allowing a single qubit to process multiple features.

questions.question: Amplitude encoding requires the data vector to be normalized.
questions.explanation: Quantum states must have unit norm, so the data vector is normalized when encoding it as amplitudes.

questions.question: Which encoding is most natural for discrete integer data?
questions.explanation: Basis encoding directly maps integers to computational basis states, e.g., 5 → |101⟩, making it natural for discrete data.

questions.question: What is the circuit depth for amplitude encoding of N features?
questions.explanation: General amplitude encoding requires O(N) gates to prepare an arbitrary N-dimensional state, making it expensive for large N.

questions.question: In angle encoding with RY gates, how is feature x typically mapped?

options.text: RY(x) where x is the raw value

questions.explanation: Features are typically scaled to [0,1] and then mapped as RY(πx) to cover the full rotation range.

questions.question: Which are valid considerations when choosing encoding? (Select all)
questions.explanation: Qubit count (a), data type (b), and circuit depth (c) are all important factors. The physical appearance of the device is irrelevant.
